title: Miscarriage — Threatened
oneLiner: Vaginal bleeding <12 weeks with closed cervix and viable intrauterine pregnancy on scan. 25% of pregnancies, usually self-limiting but requires exclusion of ectopic and molar.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy:** Unilateral iliac fossa pain + cervical excitation + free fluid in Pouch of Douglas on bedside ultrasound. If unstable → crash call + immediate transfer to theatre. Do not wait for serum hCG doubling time.
- **Septic miscarriage:** Fever >38°C, foul-smelling discharge, uterine tenderness, systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate). Requires urgent broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) and surgical evacuation within 6 hours if products retained.
- **Massive haemorrhage from inevitable/incomplete miscarriage:** Heavy soaking through pad <1/hour, haemodynamic instability, large clots. Activate massive haemorrhage protocol + IV access ×2 + crossmatch 4 units. Immediate speculum exam and consider suction evacuation.
sections.Presentation:
- Vaginal bleeding varying from spotting to heavy (fresh red or brown)
- Mild, cramping lower abdominal pain (often midline, suprapubic)
- No history of passage of tissue or clots (if tissue passed → think incomplete/complete miscarriage)
- Initially closed cervical os on speculum exam (key defining feature)
- Usually <12 weeks’ gestation; 80% will settle spontaneously
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Ectopic pregnancy:** Unilateral pain, cervical excitation. *Splitter:* **Transvaginal ultrasound** shows empty uterine cavity + adnexal mass. No intrauterine gestational sac when hCG >1500 IU/L (discriminatory zone).
- **Complete miscarriage:** History of heavy bleeding with passage of tissue, now settling. Scan shows empty uterus (endometrial thickness <15mm) with negative hCG falling.
- **Incomplete miscarriage:** Ongoing heavy bleeding, open cervical os, scan shows retained products of conception.
- **Molar pregnancy:** Bleeding + excessive vomiting (hyperemesis), "snowstorm" appearance on ultrasound, very high hCG (>100,000). Check histology post-evacuation.
- **Cervical/ectopic varices or cancer:** Uncommon; visible lesion on speculum exam, contact bleeding.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Glasgow Coma Scale, vital signs (NEWS2), speculum exam (assess cervical os; if open → inevitable/incomplete; if closed → threatened).  
  - Urine pregnancy test (if not already done) — but almost always positive.  
  - Bedside ultrasound if available: check for intrauterine gestational sac, fetal pole, fetal heart activity; look for free fluid.
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (Hb baseline, WCC for infection), group & screen (rhesus status mandatory if bleeding).  
  - Serum hCG: serial levels at 48h (normal rise ~66% if viable; suboptimal rise → ectopic or failure).  
  - If infection suspected: CRP, blood cultures, lactate.
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Transvaginal ultrasound** (TVUS) by trained sonographer. Confirm intrauterine pregnancy. Document fetal heart activity if present (good prognostic sign). Measure crown-rump length (6w+).  
  - Second-line: Transabdominal if TVUS contraindicated (e.g., severe pain).  
  - If scan inconclusive (e.g., empty uterus, hCG >1500): repeat hCG in 48h and urgent gynaecology review for possible ectopic.
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Anti-D prophylaxis:** All Rhesus-negative women with threatened miscarriage and any bleeding >12 weeks (NICE recommends anti-D from 12 weeks; some local policies from first trimester — check local policy).  
  - **HSG/hysteroscopy:** Not in acute setting.
- **Never-miss test:** **Transvaginal ultrasound (with hCG)** to confirm intrauterine pregnancy — omitting this risks missing ectopic rupture with catastrophic haemorrhage.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - ABC, IV access (wide-bore if heavy bleeding).  
  - Resuscitate with crystalloids or crossmatched blood if unstable.  
  - Urgent ultrasound.  
  - If rhesus negative and >12 weeks (or per local policy): **Anti-D 250 IU IM** within 72h (check local dose; some use 500 IU after 20 weeks).  
  - Admission if heavy bleeding, pain uncontrolled, or cannot rule out ectopic.  
  - If mild bleeding, stable, viable pregnancy on scan → may discharge with safety netting (see below).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Serial hCG + TVUS to confirm ongoing viability (normal rise).  
  - Provide reassurance; 80% settle.  
  - Avoid intercourse until bleeding stops.  
  - No progesterone therapy advised (NICE recommends against).  
  - If bleeding persists or increases → repeat scan to check for inevitable/incomplete miscarriage.  
  - If pain severe → analgesia (paracetamol 1g QDS, ibuprofen 400mg TDS with food if no contraindication; avoid if bleeding heavy due to potential antiplatelet effect — use codeine 30mg PO PRN as alternative [check local policy]).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If resolved → no specific follow-up required; advise to return if bleeding recurs.  
  - If repeated threatened miscarriages → refer to recurrent miscarriage clinic (≥3 losses).  
  - Consider early pregnancy assessment unit (EPAU) for reassurance scan at 8-10 weeks.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: If haemodynamically stable, no need unless NBM for surgery. If unstable → Hartmann’s 500ml stat, reassess.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if admitted >24h and no bleeding contraindication.  
  - Diet: Light diet; keep well hydrated.  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable; hourly if any bleeding or pain.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - *Anti-D immunoglobulin:* 250 IU IM (single dose) for threatened miscarriage >12 weeks (or per local protocol).  
  - *Analgesia:* Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day).  
  - *Antiemetics* if needed: Cyclizine 50mg PO TDS or metoclopramide 10mg TDS.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Ibuprofen 400mg TDS PO (if no contraindication — avoid in ulcer, asthma, renal impairment, third trimester).  
  - Codeine 30-60mg PO PRN (avoid if risk of constipation; use only short-term).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: Contraindicated if bleeding heavy or gastropathy.  
  - Anti-D: Do not give to Rh-positive women.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None specific for these drugs at typical doses.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  **Anti-D — giving wrong dose or time.** Must be given within 72h of bleeding onset. Overdose rare but underdose fails to prevent Rh sensitisation. Check gestational age for correct dose (local policy).  
  **Analgesia — prescribing NSAIDs in active heavy bleeding may worsen bleeding risk.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Progression to incomplete/complete miscarriage (20% progress).  
  - Haemorrhagic shock (rare in threatened, more in inevitable).  
  - Infection (endometritis) if bleeding persists.  
  - Rh sensitisation if missed anti-D prophylaxis.  
  - Ectopic misdiagnosis (if ultrasound not definitive) → rupture.
- **Chronic:**  
  - Psychological distress, anxiety, grief.  
  - Recurrent miscarriage (even one loss increases risk for next pregnancy).  
  - Asherman syndrome (rare, only if repeated curettage).
sections.Prognosis: - 80-90% of threatened miscarriages with a viable fetal heart on scan will continue to term. If subchorionic haematoma present, slightly worse (60-70%). Loss rates highest in first 8 weeks.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check Rh status** in any bleeding in pregnancy; give anti-D before 72h if Rh-negative and beyond local threshold (usually 12 weeks).  
- **Perform speculum exam** to confirm closed os; this is key to diagnosing threatened vs inevitable/incomplete.  
- **Document fetal heart activity** on ultrasound report — a normal heart rate (110-160 bpm) strongly predicts viability.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not discharge** without confirming intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound — risk of missed ectopic.  
- **Do not prescribe progesterone** as per NICE; it is not indicated.  
- **Do not assume all bleeding + pain equals threatened miscarriage** — always consider ectopic and molar.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** bleeding soaks >1 pad per hour, passes clots/tissue, develops severe unilateral pain, fever >38°C, or syncope.  
- **Follow-up:** Early pregnancy unit review in 48h for repeat hCG and scan if needed. If stable and fetus viable, routine antenatal care.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/reg immediately if:**  
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >110, NEWS2 >5) → suspect ruptured ectopic or massive haemorrhage.  
  - Severe unilateral pain or cervical excitation → rule out ectopic.  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or falling Hb >10 g/L in hours.  
  - Fever + uterine tenderness (septic miscarriage).  
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - GCS <13, respiratory rate >30, or hypotension not responding to fluid bolus.

rawMarkdown:
## Miscarriage — Threatened
**One-liner:** Vaginal bleeding <12 weeks with closed cervix and viable intrauterine pregnancy on scan. 25% of pregnancies, usually self-limiting but requires exclusion of ectopic and molar.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured ectopic pregnancy:** Unilateral iliac fossa pain + cervical excitation + free fluid in Pouch of Douglas on bedside ultrasound. If unstable → crash call + immediate transfer to theatre. Do not wait for serum hCG doubling time.
- **Septic miscarriage:** Fever >38°C, foul-smelling discharge, uterine tenderness, systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, hypotension, raised lactate). Requires urgent broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) and surgical evacuation within 6 hours if products retained.
- **Massive haemorrhage from inevitable/incomplete miscarriage:** Heavy soaking through pad <1/hour, haemodynamic instability, large clots. Activate massive haemorrhage protocol + IV access ×2 + crossmatch 4 units. Immediate speculum exam and consider suction evacuation.

### Presentation
- Vaginal bleeding varying from spotting to heavy (fresh red or brown)
- Mild, cramping lower abdominal pain (often midline, suprapubic)
- No history of passage of tissue or clots (if tissue passed → think incomplete/complete miscarriage)
- Initially closed cervical os on speculum exam (key defining feature)
- Usually <12 weeks’ gestation; 80% will settle spontaneously

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Ectopic pregnancy:** Unilateral pain, cervical excitation. *Splitter:* **Transvaginal ultrasound** shows empty uterine cavity + adnexal mass. No intrauterine gestational sac when hCG >1500 IU/L (discriminatory zone).
- **Complete miscarriage:** History of heavy bleeding with passage of tissue, now settling. Scan shows empty uterus (endometrial thickness <15mm) with negative hCG falling.
- **Incomplete miscarriage:** Ongoing heavy bleeding, open cervical os, scan shows retained products of conception.
- **Molar pregnancy:** Bleeding + excessive vomiting (hyperemesis), "snowstorm" appearance on ultrasound, very high hCG (>100,000). Check histology post-evacuation.
- **Cervical/ectopic varices or cancer:** Uncommon; visible lesion on speculum exam, contact bleeding.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Glasgow Coma Scale, vital signs (NEWS2), speculum exam (assess cervical os; if open → inevitable/incomplete; if closed → threatened).  
  - Urine pregnancy test (if not already done) — but almost always positive.  
  - Bedside ultrasound if available: check for intrauterine gestational sac, fetal pole, fetal heart activity; look for free fluid.
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC (Hb baseline, WCC for infection), group & screen (rhesus status mandatory if bleeding).  
  - Serum hCG: serial levels at 48h (normal rise ~66% if viable; suboptimal rise → ectopic or failure).  
  - If infection suspected: CRP, blood cultures, lactate.
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Transvaginal ultrasound** (TVUS) by trained sonographer. Confirm intrauterine pregnancy. Document fetal heart activity if present (good prognostic sign). Measure crown-rump length (6w+).  
  - Second-line: Transabdominal if TVUS contraindicated (e.g., severe pain).  
  - If scan inconclusive (e.g., empty uterus, hCG >1500): repeat hCG in 48h and urgent gynaecology review for possible ectopic.
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Anti-D prophylaxis:** All Rhesus-negative women with threatened miscarriage and any bleeding >12 weeks (NICE recommends anti-D from 12 weeks; some local policies from first trimester — check local policy).  
  - **HSG/hysteroscopy:** Not in acute setting.
- **Never-miss test:** **Transvaginal ultrasound (with hCG)** to confirm intrauterine pregnancy — omitting this risks missing ectopic rupture with catastrophic haemorrhage.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - ABC, IV access (wide-bore if heavy bleeding).  
  - Resuscitate with crystalloids or crossmatched blood if unstable.  
  - Urgent ultrasound.  
  - If rhesus negative and >12 weeks (or per local policy): **Anti-D 250 IU IM** within 72h (check local dose; some use 500 IU after 20 weeks).  
  - Admission if heavy bleeding, pain uncontrolled, or cannot rule out ectopic.  
  - If mild bleeding, stable, viable pregnancy on scan → may discharge with safety netting (see below).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Serial hCG + TVUS to confirm ongoing viability (normal rise).  
  - Provide reassurance; 80% settle.  
  - Avoid intercourse until bleeding stops.  
  - No progesterone therapy advised (NICE recommends against).  
  - If bleeding persists or increases → repeat scan to check for inevitable/incomplete miscarriage.  
  - If pain severe → analgesia (paracetamol 1g QDS, ibuprofen 400mg TDS with food if no contraindication; avoid if bleeding heavy due to potential antiplatelet effect — use codeine 30mg PO PRN as alternative [check local policy]).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - If resolved → no specific follow-up required; advise to return if bleeding recurs.  
  - If repeated threatened miscarriages → refer to recurrent miscarriage clinic (≥3 losses).  
  - Consider early pregnancy assessment unit (EPAU) for reassurance scan at 8-10 weeks.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: If haemodynamically stable, no need unless NBM for surgery. If unstable → Hartmann’s 500ml stat, reassess.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: Low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if admitted >24h and no bleeding contraindication.  
  - Diet: Light diet; keep well hydrated.  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly if stable; hourly if any bleeding or pain.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - *Anti-D immunoglobulin:* 250 IU IM (single dose) for threatened miscarriage >12 weeks (or per local protocol).  
  - *Analgesia:* Paracetamol 1g PO QDS (max 4g/day).  
  - *Antiemetics* if needed: Cyclizine 50mg PO TDS or metoclopramide 10mg TDS.  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Ibuprofen 400mg TDS PO (if no contraindication — avoid in ulcer, asthma, renal impairment, third trimester).  
  - Codeine 30-60mg PO PRN (avoid if risk of constipation; use only short-term).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs: Contraindicated if bleeding heavy or gastropathy.  
  - Anti-D: Do not give to Rh-positive women.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None specific for these drugs at typical doses.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  **Anti-D — giving wrong dose or time.** Must be given within 72h of bleeding onset. Overdose rare but underdose fails to prevent Rh sensitisation. Check gestational age for correct dose (local policy).  
  **Analgesia — prescribing NSAIDs in active heavy bleeding may worsen bleeding risk.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Progression to incomplete/complete miscarriage (20% progress).  
  - Haemorrhagic shock (rare in threatened, more in inevitable).  
  - Infection (endometritis) if bleeding persists.  
  - Rh sensitisation if missed anti-D prophylaxis.  
  - Ectopic misdiagnosis (if ultrasound not definitive) → rupture.
- **Chronic:**  
  - Psychological distress, anxiety, grief.  
  - Recurrent miscarriage (even one loss increases risk for next pregnancy).  
  - Asherman syndrome (rare, only if repeated curettage).

### Prognosis
- 80-90% of threatened miscarriages with a viable fetal heart on scan will continue to term. If subchorionic haematoma present, slightly worse (60-70%). Loss rates highest in first 8 weeks.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check Rh status** in any bleeding in pregnancy; give anti-D before 72h if Rh-negative and beyond local threshold (usually 12 weeks).  
- **Perform speculum exam** to confirm closed os; this is key to diagnosing threatened vs inevitable/incomplete.  
- **Document fetal heart activity** on ultrasound report — a normal heart rate (110-160 bpm) strongly predicts viability.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not discharge** without confirming intrauterine pregnancy on ultrasound — risk of missed ectopic.  
- **Do not prescribe progesterone** as per NICE; it is not indicated.  
- **Do not assume all bleeding + pain equals threatened miscarriage** — always consider ectopic and molar.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** bleeding soaks >1 pad per hour, passes clots/tissue, develops severe unilateral pain, fever >38°C, or syncope.  
- **Follow-up:** Early pregnancy unit review in 48h for repeat hCG and scan if needed. If stable and fetus viable, routine antenatal care.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/reg immediately if:**  
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >110, NEWS2 >5) → suspect ruptured ectopic or massive haemorrhage.  
  - Severe unilateral pain or cervical excitation → rule out ectopic.  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or falling Hb >10 g/L in hours.  
  - Fever + uterine tenderness (septic miscarriage).  
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - GCS <13, respiratory rate >30, or hypotension not responding to fluid bolus.
